detailedExemplarData.title: In what ways does Carol Ann Duffy reinterpret female identities through âMrs. Quasimodoâ, âPilateâs Wifeâ and âMedusaâ in âThe Worldâs Wife?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively with a clear, sustained focus on Duffyâs reinterpretation of female identities.
The research question is explicitly stated, suitably narrow, and consistently connected to the analysis throughout the essay.
Minor lapses in expression do not undermine the overall coherence and effectiveness of the methodology section.
weaknesses: The methodology lacks an explicit discussion of source-selection criteria and wider contextual grounding.
Some sections drift into summary rather than methodological reflection, limiting evidence of informed selection.
Occasional grammatical slips and repetition slightly reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessments place the student solidly at the upper end of the 3â4 band for methodology (scored 4) and at 5 for topic clarity and research-question focus.  Taken together, these justify a holistic Focus and Method score of 5/6: the work demonstrates a clear purpose, focused question, and generally effective methodological approach, tempered by a need for more explicit methodological reflection.

strengths: Sound knowledge of the three poems with relevant thematic identification and some integration of feminist criticism.
Appropriate application of literary terminology such as âimagery,â âmetaphor,â and âtone.â
weaknesses: Knowledge occasionally slips into descriptive paraphrase and contains factual imprecision.
Use of terminology is inconsistent and sometimes inaccurate (e.g., mislabelling poetic genres, literalising figurative language).
Heavy reliance on non-scholarly online annotations weakens the depth and credibility of source use.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 4 (knowledge of topic) and 3 (use of terminology) reflect a student who demonstrates good but uneven knowledge and only partially effective application of sources.  A holistic score of 4/6 accurately captures this mid-range performance.

strengths: Clear thematic commentary supported by well-chosen quotations showing ability to analyse poetic techniques.
Evidence of synthesis in the comparative discussion and emerging critical perspective on Duffyâs feminist message.
Relevant research sources consulted and integrated with moderate effectiveness.
weaknesses: Analysis sometimes slides into narrative summary and lacks systematic treatment of alternative interpretations.
Evaluation is largely assertive rather than critically reflective; counter-arguments are absent.
Conclusion restates earlier points without offering fresh insights or limitations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each of the three Critical Thinking strands was placed in the 4â6 band (adequate to good).  While the analysis and evaluation show notable strengths, the absence of deeper critical reflection and alternative viewpoints keeps the work out of the top band.  Awarding 6/12 situates the essay in the middle of the 4â6 band, acknowledging solid analytical work and relevance but also its limitations.

strengths: All required structural elements (title, headings, pagination, bibliography) are present and reader navigation is effective.
Layout is functional and generally reader-friendly, with a clear Table of Contents.
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in in-text citation style and typographical errors detract from polish.
Some section transitions are abrupt, and headings occasionally lack refinement.
Visual details (dot leaders, alignment) and grammatical slips reduce overall presentation quality.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Presentation strands both fell into the 3â4 band.  Despite minor lapses in referencing style and typographical precision, the essay meets all core structural and formatting requirements.  A score of 3/4 appropriately reflects this level of performance.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The methodologyâclose textual analysis supported by secondary literary criticismâis **generally appropriate and mostly complete**. The student consults pertinent feminist theorists (Butler, Gilbert & Gubar) and critical essays, and includes a bibliography with books and articles.  
Yet, the essay relies heavily on readily available online annotations (Genius) and offers **limited explanation of selection criteria or research process**; there is no explicit discussion of how sources were identified or evaluated, nor evidence of wider primary/secondary breadth (e.g., historical context, scholarly journals beyond a few citations).  
Consequently, while the chosen methods are serviceable and relevant, the demonstration of informed and effective selection is only partial. This aligns with the upper end of the 3â4 band, meriting a 4.

Basic EE formatting requirements are met: **word count, title, page numbers, section headings and a bibliography** are provided, and the table of contents matches pagination reasonably well.  
There are, however, lapses in academic referencing style (in-text citations lack full consistency; some URLs are incomplete), and typographical errors mar overall polish. Even so, layout remains functional and reader-friendly, satisfying the criteria for the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects these minor shortcomings.

The student consults a **modest set of sources**: two scholarly monographs, an academic article and several public online annotations. These materials are relevant to feminist readings of Duffy and the specific poems, demonstrating adequate research reach.  
However, the bibliography is limited for an EE (no peer-reviewed journal articles beyond one, no primary textual editions, little engagement with wider critical debate). Citations within the essay are sparse and sometimes incomplete, which **reduces the reliability and demonstrable relevance** of the research base.  
Thus, the work meets the descriptors for the 4â6 band but at a basic level, earning a 4.

The studentâs title and opening paragraphs **clearly identify a focused literary topicâDuffyâs re-interpretation of three specified poems from The Worldâs Wifeâand explain its feminist significance.** The introduction situates Duffy in her historical and literary context, outlines key critical perspectives and states the aim of examining how female identities are re-imagined.  
â¢ **Purpose and focus remain apparent** across the essay: each body section is headed by the poemâs title and explicitly links back to the notion of âreinterpretation of female identities.â  
â¢ Minor issues of expression (e.g., occasional grammatical slips and some repetition) slightly blur precision, yet overall communication of the topic is sustained and effective. These small lapses prevent the exemplary cohesion required for the top of the band, warranting a strong but not maximum score of 5.

The student develops a **coherent line of argument** about Duffyâs feminist reinterpretation and offers a brief comparative discussion that attempts to evaluate the poetâs success. Some reflective comments (e.g., on the significance of Duffyâs work to modern feminist writing) show emerging critical perspective.  
Yet evaluation is **largely assertive**; it rarely questions Duffyâs choices, engages opposing viewpoints, or reflects on limitations. The conclusion reiterates earlier points rather than offering fresh insight, and the essayâs structure occasionally leads to repetitive commentary rather than progressive development.  
Thus, while the discussion moves beyond description, its critical depth is moderate, matching the lower end of the 4â6 band; a score of 4 is appropriate.

The essay provides **clear thematic commentary and some textual evidence** (quoted lines followed by interpretive remarks) that show an ability to analyse poetic techniques and their effect on character portrayal. Comparative observations in the later section reveal an attempt at synthesis.  
Nonetheless, analysis often **slides into narrative summary**; many claims about empowerment or patriarchal critique are asserted rather than thoroughly demonstrated. Close reading is selective and sometimes superficial, and counter-arguments or alternative interpretations are absent.  
The conclusions drawn are partially supported, fitting the âadequate analysisâ description. Hence a score of 4 within the 4â6 band is justified.

The essay includes all required structural elements: **title page, abstract substitute (intro), table of contents, clearly labelled sections, conclusion and bibliography.** The sequence is logical and facilitates reader navigation.  
However, minor inconsistencies existâoccasional abrupt section transitions, uneven paragraphing and an underdeveloped methodology sectionâindicating room for improved organisation. These issues place the work at the lower end of the âclearly appropriateâ band, warranting a 3.

The essay shows **sound knowledge of the three poems**, summarising narrative content and identifying themes of marginalisation, jealousy and empowerment. References to feminist critics (Rees-Jones, Ramsay) and theorists (Butler) indicate engagement with secondary scholarship, and quotations from the poems are woven into the discussion.  
However, the knowledge occasionally slips into **descriptive paraphrase**; some contextual remarks (e.g., Duffy âfirst womenâ Laureate) are factually imprecise and the critical material, while relevant, is treated at a surface level. Application of sources supports points but is not consistently integrated to build a sophisticated argument.  
Thus, the work meets the descriptors for **good but uneven knowledge and partially effective use of sources**, deserving a 4 within the 3â4 band.

The student employs literary terminology such as **âfirst-person narrative,â âfree verse,â âimagery,â âmetaphor,â âtone,â and âsymbolism,â** indicating an awareness of subject-specific concepts.  
Yet usage is **inconsistent and occasionally inaccurate** (e.g., calling the anthology âfiction,â mislabelling âself-mutilationâ rather than figurative silencing, awkward phrasing around poetic structure). Some technical terms (e.g., âtetrahedron noseâ) are lifted from the poem without analytical precision.  
Overall, terminology is mostly correct but not always applied with confidence or nuance, fitting comfortably in the mid-range of the 3â4 band, so a score of 3 is appropriate.

The research questionââIn what ways does Carol Ann Duffy reinterpret female identities through âMrs Quasimodo,â âPilateâs Wifeâ and âMedusaâ in The Worldâs Wife?ââis **explicit, focused and suitably narrow**, restricting investigation to three poems and a single guiding concept (female identity).  
Throughout the essay, each analytical section re-states or alludes to this central question, indicating a **consistent line of inquiry**. However, the linkage between some paragraphs and the research question occasionally drifts into plot summary rather than sustained exploration, showing that the focus, while clear, is not always maintained with the utmost precision; hence a score of 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The label âStudent Code:â appears without the actual code; include the student identifier to meet formatting requirements.
The word count is provided and falls within the 4 000-word limit, demonstrating compliance with formatting rules.
The repeated heading âCategory 1â is extraneous and may confuse readers; refine section titles for clarity.
No theoretical framework (e.g., Butlerâs gender performativity) is integrated here; situate your analysis within a clear methodological lens.
The comparative discussion remains general; structure it systematically around thematic, formal and theoretical points for stronger evaluation.
Your thematic identification of guilt and cooperation in Pilateâs Wife shows thoughtful engagement with the poemâs moral issues.
Analysis of patriarchal power is appropriate but could be reinforced by examining stanza breaks or enjambment reflective of her constraint.
The phrase âallusion and historical allusionsâ is redundant; refine wording to eliminate repetition for greater stylistic precision.
The sentence âher voice enables to expressâ is grammatically unclear; revise for subjectâverb agreement and clarity.
The transition into the Medusa section lacks a linking sentence; add a brief comparative segue to improve flow.
The section heading âAnalysis of Mrs. Quasimodoâ clearly signals your analytical focus and aids the readerâs orientation.
Identifying the first-person narrative and tone here demonstrates an appropriate initial close-reading approach.
The phrase âI felt a thump of confidenceâ invites sound imagery analysisâconsider linking onomatopoeia to her emotional state for richer interpretation.
This paragraph ends abruptly mid-quote; ensure complete sentences to maintain analytical coherence and readability.
The title and research question are clearly stated and tightly focused, establishing a coherent purpose and guiding the analysis throughout the essay.
The Table of Contents is clearly presented, accurately reflecting section titles and page numbers for ease of navigation.
Consider aligning the dot leaders and page numerals in the contents table for greater visual consistency.
The âIntroductionâ heading is clearly labelled, establishing the beginning of your argument effectively.
There is a grammatical slip: âfirst womenâ should read âfirst woman.â Correcting this will enhance precision and professionalism.
Restating the research question is helpful, but you should integrate methodological reflection on how each poem was chosen and analyzed.
The claim that the poem âdoesn't have a rhythmâ is too absolute; free verse still exhibits rhythmic patternsâidentify specific metrical choices instead.
The introduction effectively cites feminist critics, but the use of their ideas remains descriptive rather than critically synthesised; deepen your engagement with these sources.
Your methodology (source selection, evaluation criteria) is not explicitly discussed here; include a concise methodology section to demonstrate informed selection.
This overview of The Worldâs Wife drifts into summary; sharpen the connection to your research question by foregrounding analytical purpose.
Your discussion of imagery is accurate but lacks deeper exploration of connotation; unpack how the bell metaphor evolves to support your feminist argument.
The articulation of Duffyâs feminist message is clear and demonstrates awareness of how narrative voice challenges patriarchal constructs.
The conclusion effectively restates your argument but omits reflection on limitations or suggestions for further research; consider adding these elements.
Referring to âMrs. Quasimodoâ as a ânovelâ is incorrectâmaintain precise terminology when discussing poetic genres.
The argument remains largely descriptive; engage more critically by considering alternative readings or counter-examples.
Good contextualization of Pilate's Wife, but cite the specific Gospel verses to strengthen your scholarly rigour.
Your close reading of Medusaâs self-loathing and transformation employs precise quotation and insightful metaphor analysis.
The discussion of imagery contrasting destruction and loss deepens the readerâs understanding of Medusaâs dual role.
Extend your critique by exploring how the gaze motif engages with feminist theories of the male gaze and power dynamics.
Your identification of distinct voices is perceptive but would benefit from direct quotation analysis in each comparative claim.
The âBibliographyâ heading is correctly formatted, providing a clear start to your reference list.
Heavy reliance on Genius annotations weakens scholarly credibility; integrate more peer-reviewed articles or primary editions.
Citation formatting is inconsistent (italics, URLs, punctuation); adopt a single style guide (MLA, APA) and apply it uniformly.
